dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for diabetic problem at address redacted in [CITY].
Good evening sir, I'm Jeff, a paramedic with Rescue Squad. What made you call 911 today?
Nausea and vomiting for two days, and my glucometer is showing HI. I'm scared I'm in DKA again.
Mr. Barron, can you tell me your name and when is your birthday please?
Eyes are open, equally and bilaterally reactive.
What county are we in and when is the date today please?
He is able to point to both sides when prompted and follow commands accordingly.
We're going to do a quick check of your airway - that okay with you?
No signs of respiratory distress, breath sounds clear bilaterally.
Pulse is strong and regular. No signs of shock or abnormal color.
How long has the vomiting lasted, and have you noticed anything else?
Two whole days. I've also been very thirsty. I tried to drink more since I did peritoneal dialysis at night, but I haven't been able to take in much due to the vomiting.
Can you tell me about your medical history and medications please?

I have diabetes, high blood pressure, heart failure, and I'm on dialysis. I take amlodipine, labetalol, Humalog, Metolazone, Ergocalciferol, atorvastatin, and torsemide among others. I don't know of any allergies.
When did your symptoms start this time around and what were you doing prior to that?
Symptoms started about two and a half days ago. I did peritoneal dialysis this morning, and it went fine as far as I know. I've mostly been on the couch since.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your current pain?
It's a 0, really. I feel some pressure from the fluid, but no actual pain.
Pulse is 92, strong and regular.
Respirations are 16, even and unlabored.
Blood pressure is 168 over 96.
Glucose is 443 - that's very high.
Mr. Barron, can I check your eyes and neck visually and shine a light into your pupils?
Of course.
Pupils are reactive with no signs of neurological deficits noted. I do see a bit of puffiness.
No signs of drainage or masses observed in your ears, nose, or throat.
May I listen to your lungs now with the stethoscope? Please take a few deep breaths.
Sure.
Lungs are clear bilaterally, and heart is regular without extrasystoles.
I'll do a quick visual check of your abdomen. Would you mind if I gently press on it?
No problem.
No significant pain on palpation, and no visible masses. Normal contour.
Skin is warm with no rashes or wounds observed.
You were moving from the couch without difficulty, but have you experienced tingling or weakness in your limbs?
No, just the puffiness again. I haven't felt anything unusual otherwise.
Oxygen saturation is at 99 on room air.
EKG shows sinus rhythm - all looks normal so far.
Based on the patient's response and findings, we'll follow the diabetic hyperglycemia protocol for transport and possible IV support.
We'll place a 20-gauge IV in your left hand to prepare for possible fluid or medication administration en route.
IV inserted with one attempt and is ready for use as needed during transport.
CV - ECG - 4 Lead Monitoring has been initiated.
Pulse remains 92, blood pressure 156 over 89, oxygen at 98. How are you feeling now?
The same - maybe a bit more comforted knowing that something is being done to monitor the blood sugar.
No significant pain still reported - just the same slight discomfort you mentioned earlier.
We'll be transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME] - Forest. Your primary hospital is on diversion.
That's fine - just make sure they can monitor this properly and get me back on track.
We're ready to transfer you to the stretcher. We'll make sure you're secure and comfortable.
Fine, just secure me up. I'm good to go.
You'll be placed on the stretcher for the safest transport. You are fully secured.
Any specific concerns of yours we should note for the ER?
No specific concerns, really. I'm more or less just ready to get checked out.
You were AAOx4, with no signs of pain noted - any changes or updates?
No new symptoms or changes since the last check. I'll make that note in the handoff report before transport.
You're now fully secured on the stretcher. All straps are in place and you're ready for transport.
Have you been feeling any shortness of breath or chest discomfort while we are preparing?
No, only slight puffiness and I feel okay, all things considered.
Just to confirm, are you having any chest pain or pressure at the moment?
No. I would have mentioned it if I had any chest issues. It's not something I'm feeling at the moment.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm - no abnormal heartbeats or irregular heart rhythms detected.
New glucose reading is showing as 0 - we'll document this for reference, as we took a prior reading.
No jugular venous distention noted. Mild swelling reported but otherwise normal neck appearance.
Good peripheral motor and sensory function in all four limbs - no changes observed.
Checking your vitals one more time for reassessment - pulse 92, blood pressure 156/89, oximetry 98 percent.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm as previously noted. No ectopy noted.
Hospital is 6.1 miles away. We will continue en route to [COMPANY_NAME] - Forest.
We'll ensure that your blood sugar is continuously being monitored for fluctuations during transport.
No new symptoms reported and vitals remain stable. We will continue en route prepared for any changes.
You're doing well, Mr. Barron. We're keeping a close eye on your condition and will provide all information to the hospital upon arrival.
Based on the ongoing assessment and vitals, no emergent treatment is needed, but we'll continue following protocol 3-7 for hospital en route care.
We're all set for transport - we'll make a quick exit and head to the hospital immediately.
